front: Opera: Verdi opera about the dying courtesan Violetta Valéry and her lover Alfredo Germont; title means 'The Fallen Woman'
Opera: based on Alexandre Dumas fils's La Dame aux Camélias; libretto by Francesco Maria Piave
Opera: originally set in the era of Richelieu at Teatro La Fenice's insistence, against the composer's wishes
Opera: after the unsuccessful premiere, the composer asked 'Was the fault mine or the singers?'
Opera: the title character is based on Marie Duplessis, who was a lover of Franz Liszt in her last year
Opera: Giorgio Germont sings 'Pura siccome un angelo' while persuading the title character to leave his son for the sake of his daughter's reputation
Opera: Alfredo throws his casino winnings at Violetta's feet in humiliation at Flora's party; Baron Douphol is injured in an offstage duel
Opera: Violetta gives Alfredo a camellia flower and tells him to return it when it wilts (a detail from the Dumas source novel)
Opera: Giorgio Germont sings 'Di Provenza il mar' ('the sea and soil of Provence') to comfort Alfredo after he reads Violetta's farewell letter
Opera: party scene features gypsy entertainers ('Noi siamo zingarelle') and a matador song about the bold Piquillo from Spain/Biscay
Opera: Carlos Kleiber's only studio recording of a non-German-language opera was this work, with Ileana Cotrubaș
Opera: Willy Decker's 2005 staging at Salzburg featured a massive clock to symbolize the title character's mortality
Opera: in the only non-sung passage, Violetta shouts 'E tardi!' while reading a letter as a carnival procession passes outside
Opera: a solo clarinet plays a repeated eighth note, quarter note, eighth note, eighth-note-rest figure as Violetta writes her farewell letter in Act 2
Opera: brindisi (drinking song) in which Alfredo and Violetta toast together at the Act 1 party after Baron Douphol refuses to give a toast
back: "Libiamo ne' lieti calici" from La Traviata (Verdi)

front: Opera: cabaletta at the end of Act 1 in which Violetta resolves to live freely ('frolic from joy to joy'), rejecting love; offstage voice sings of love while she sings
Opera: preceded by 'Ah, fors' è lui,' in which Violetta wonders if Alfredo was 'the one'
Opera: Act 1 duet in which Alfredo declares love ('torture, torture and delight'); Violetta offers only friendship and gives him a camellia
back: "Un dì, felice, eterea" from La Traviata (Verdi)
